Giacobbe LaMotta (1922-07-10)July 10, 1922  [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), U.S.

"**Jake**" **LaMotta** (July 10, 1922 – September 19, 2017) was an American [professional boxer](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") who was world [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight "Middleweight") champion between 1949 and 1951. Nicknamed "**the Bronx Bull**" or "**Raging Bull**" for his technique of constant stalking, brawling and inside fighting, he developed a reputation for being a "bully"; he was what is often referred to today as a [swarmer](/wiki/Boxing_styles_and_technique#The_Swarmer "Boxing styles and technique") and a [slugger](/wiki/Boxing_styles_and_technique#The_Slugger "Boxing styles and technique").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

**Giacobbe** "**Jake**" **LaMotta** (July 10, 1922 – September 19, 2017) was an American [professional boxer](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") who was world [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight "Middleweight") champion between 1949 and 1951. Nicknamed "**the Bronx Bull**" or "**Raging Bull**" for his technique of constant stalking, brawling and inside fighting, he developed a reputation for being a "bully"; he was what is often referred to today as a [swarmer](/wiki/Boxing_styles_and_technique#The_Swarmer "Boxing styles and technique") and a [slugger](/wiki/Boxing_styles_and_technique#The_Slugger "Boxing styles and technique").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Due to his hard style of fighting, LaMotta often took as much as he dished out in an era of great middleweights. With a thick skull and jaw muscles, LaMotta was able to absorb incredible amounts of punishment over the course of his career, and is thought to have one of the greatest [chins](/wiki/Chin_(boxing) "Chin (boxing)") in boxing history. LaMotta's [six-fight rivalry with Sugar Ray Robinson](/wiki/Sugar_Ray_Robinson_vs._Jake_LaMotta "Sugar Ray Robinson vs. Jake LaMotta") was one of the most notable in the sport. Although each fight was close and LaMotta dropped Robinson to the canvas multiple times, LaMotta won only one of the bouts. He was also awarded an early version of the [Hickok Belt](/wiki/Hickok_Belt#History "Hickok Belt")—then presented exclusively to boxers—in 1949 after capturing the world middleweight title.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

LaMotta, who lived a turbulent life in and out of the ring, was managed by his brother [Joey](/wiki/Joey_LaMotta "Joey LaMotta"). He was ranked 52nd on *[Ring Magazine](/wiki/Ring_Magazine "Ring Magazine")*'s list of the "80 Best Fighters of the Last 80 Years",[[5]](#cite_note-5) and also ranked amongst its list of the 10 greatest middleweights of all time.[[6]](#cite_note-6) LaMotta was inducted into the [International Boxing Hall of Fame](/wiki/International_Boxing_Hall_of_Fame "International Boxing Hall of Fame") in the inaugural class of 1990.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

LaMotta's autobiography was adapted into the 1980 [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") film *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")*. It went on to be nominated for [eight Academy Awards](/wiki/53rd_Academy_Awards "53rd Academy Awards"), with [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro") winning [Best Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor") for his portrayal of LaMotta.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

LaMotta was born on the [Lower East Side](/wiki/Lower_East_Side "Lower East Side") of New York City on July 10, 1922, to Italian parents, Elizabeth (Merluzzo) and Giuseppe "Joseph" LaMotta.[[8]](#cite_note-nyt-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) Many sources had reported his year of birth as 1921,[[11]](#cite_note-ausobit-11) but his daughter Christi claimed it was in fact 1922.[[11]](#cite_note-ausobit-11) His mother was born in the United States to Italian immigrants, while his father was an immigrant from [Messina](/wiki/Messina "Messina"), Sicily, who came with family including Jake's older brother Joseph. The family lived briefly in Philadelphia before returning to New York City and settling in the [Bronx](/wiki/Bronx "Bronx").[[8]](#cite_note-nyt-8)

Jake's father forced the boy to fight other boys in order to entertain neighborhood adults, who threw pocket change into the ring. LaMotta's father collected the money and used it to help pay the rent.[[12]](#cite_note-AftertheFall-12) One of LaMotta's cousins on his father's side was [Richard LaMotta](/wiki/Richard_LaMotta "Richard LaMotta"), who became an entrepreneur and creator of the [Chipwich](/wiki/Chipwich "Chipwich") ice cream treat.[[13]](#cite_note-NYTOBITUARY-13)

LaMotta learned to box while in a [reformatory](/wiki/Reformatory "Reformatory") in upstate New York, where he'd been sent for attempted robbery.[[8]](#cite_note-nyt-8) Afterward he fought undefeated in amateur bouts, turning professional at age 19 in 1941. During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), he was rejected for military service due to a childhood [mastoid operation](/wiki/Mastoid_process "Mastoid process") on one of his ears which affected his hearing.[[8]](#cite_note-nyt-8)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Boxing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Boxing career")]

As a [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight "Middleweight") in his first fifteen bouts, LaMotta went 14–0–1 (3 KOs). On September 24, 1941, he almost defeated [Jimmy Reeves](/wiki/Jimmy_Reeves "Jimmy Reeves") by a knockout in Reeves' hometown of [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), Ohio, but the referee counted to nine before he was interrupted by the boxing bell, signaling the end of the match, and by a split decision, LaMotta controversially lost to Reeves, despite the latter being knocked down. Chaos erupted after the decision was announced. Fights broke out around the ring and the crowd continued to riot for 20 minutes. The arena's organist unsuccessfully tried to calm down the crowd by playing the "[Star Spangled Banner](/wiki/Star_Spangled_Banner "Star Spangled Banner")". However, while the crowd continued to riot, the violence outside the ring led to many arrests.

One month later, LaMotta and Reeves fought again in the same arena. LaMotta lost a much less controversial decision. A third match between the two took place on March 19, 1943, in Detroit, Michigan. The first five rounds were close, though Reeves was struggling in the fourth. In the sixth round, LaMotta floored Reeves, who was down for only a second. Once the fight resumed, LaMotta landed a left on Reeves' chin, sending him down face-first. Reeves was blinking his eyes and shaking his head as the referee counted him out.

### LaMotta vs. Robinson I–V

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: LaMotta vs. Robinson I–V")]

See also: [Sugar Ray Robinson vs. Jake LaMotta](/wiki/Sugar_Ray_Robinson_vs._Jake_LaMotta "Sugar Ray Robinson vs. Jake LaMotta")

LaMotta fought [Sugar Ray Robinson](/wiki/Sugar_Ray_Robinson "Sugar Ray Robinson") in Robinson's middleweight debut at [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden_(1925) "Madison Square Garden (1925)"), New York City, October 2, 1942.[[15]](#cite_note-Sweet_Thunder-15) LaMotta knocked Robinson down in the first round of the fight. Robinson got up and took control over much of the fight, winning via a unanimous 10-round decision.[[15]](#cite_note-Sweet_Thunder-15)

A 10-round rematch took place February 5, 1943, at [Olympia Stadium](/wiki/Olympia_Stadium,_Detroit "Olympia Stadium, Detroit") in Detroit, Michigan.[[15]](#cite_note-Sweet_Thunder-15) In the eighth round, LaMotta landed a right to Robinson's head and a left to his body, sending him through the ropes. Robinson was saved by the bell at the count of nine. LaMotta, who was already leading on the scorecards before knocking Robinson out of the ring, pummeled and [outpointed](/wiki/Boxing_scoring "Boxing scoring") him for the rest of the fight. Robinson had trouble keeping LaMotta at bay.[[16]](#cite_note-16) LaMotta won via unanimous decision, giving Robinson the first defeat of his career.

The victory was short-lived, as the two met on February 26, 1943, in what was another 10-round fight, once again at Olympia Stadium in Robinson's former home of Detroit.[[15]](#cite_note-Sweet_Thunder-15) Robinson was knocked down for a nine-count in Round 7. Robinson later stated, "He really hurt me with a left in the seventh round. I was a little dazed and decided to stay on the deck." Robinson won the close fight by unanimous decision, using a dazzling left jab and jarring uppercuts.[[17]](#cite_note-17) LaMotta said the fight was given to Robinson because he would be inducted into the army the next day.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

A fourth fight, the duo's final 10 rounder, took place nearly two years after the third, on February 23, 1945, at Madison Square Garden, New York.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Robinson won again by a unanimous decision.

LaMotta and Robinson had their fifth bout at [Comiskey Park](/wiki/Comiskey_Park "Comiskey Park"), Chicago, Illinois on September 26, 1945. Robinson won by a very controversial split decision, contested over 12 rounds.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The decision was severely booed by the 14,755 people in attendance. LaMotta later said in his autobiography that the decision was widely criticized by several newspapers and boxing publishers. Robinson said afterward, "This was the toughest fight I've ever had with LaMotta."[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### LaMotta vs. Fox

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: LaMotta vs. Fox")]

On November 14, 1947, LaMotta was knocked out in the fourth round by [Billy Fox](/wiki/Billy_Fox_(boxer) "Billy Fox (boxer)"). Suspecting the fight was [fixed](/wiki/Match_fixing "Match fixing"), the [New York State Athletic Commission](/wiki/New_York_State_Athletic_Commission "New York State Athletic Commission") withheld purses for the fight and suspended LaMotta. The fight with Fox would come back to haunt him later in life, during a case with the [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation").

In his testimony and in his later book, LaMotta admitted to throwing the fight to gain favor with the [Mafia](/wiki/Italian-American_Mafia "Italian-American Mafia"). All involved agreed the fix was obvious and their staging inept.

As LaMotta wrote,

> The first round, a couple of belts to his head, and I see a glassy look coming over his eyes. Jesus Christ, a couple of jabs and he's going to fall down? I began to panic a little. I was supposed to be throwing a fight to this guy, and it looked like I was going to end up holding him on his feet... By [the fourth round], if there was anybody in the Garden who didn't know what was happening, he must have been dead drunk.[[22]](#cite_note-ReelLife-22)

The thrown fight and a payment of $20,000 to the Mafia got LaMotta his title bout against World Middleweight Champion [Marcel Cerdan](/wiki/Marcel_Cerdan "Marcel Cerdan").[[23]](#cite_note-Congress-23)

### LaMotta vs. Cerdan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: LaMotta vs. Cerdan")]

LaMotta won the World Middleweight title on June 16, 1949, in Detroit, Michigan, defeating [Frenchman](/wiki/French_people "French people") [Marcel Cerdan](/wiki/Marcel_Cerdan "Marcel Cerdan").[[24]](#cite_note-24) LaMotta won the first round (in which he knocked Cerdan down), Cerdan the second, and the third was even. At that point it became clear something was wrong. Cerdan dislocated his arm in the first round, apparently damaged in the knockdown, and gave up before the start of the 10th round. LaMotta damaged his left hand in the fifth round, but still landed 104 punches in the ninth round, whereas Cerdan hardly threw a punch.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The official score had LaMotta as winner by a knockout in 10 rounds because the bell had already rung to begin that round when Cerdan announced he was quitting. A rematch was arranged, but while Cerdan was flying back to the United States to fight the rematch, his [Air France Lockheed Constellation crashed](/wiki/1949_Air_France_Lockheed_Constellation_crash "1949 Air France Lockheed Constellation crash") in the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores"), killing everyone on board.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### World Middleweight Champion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: World Middleweight Champion")]

LaMotta made his first title defense against [Tiberio Mitri](/wiki/Tiberio_Mitri "Tiberio Mitri") on July 7, 1950, at Madison Square Garden, New York. LaMotta retained his title via unanimous decision. LaMotta's next defense came on September 13, 1950, against [Laurent Dauthuille](/wiki/Laurent_Dauthuille "Laurent Dauthuille"). Dauthuille had previously beaten LaMotta by decision before LaMotta became world champion. By the fifteenth round, Dauthuille was ahead on all scorecards (72–68, 74–66, 71–69) and seemed to be about to repeat a victory against LaMotta. LaMotta hit Dauthuille with a barrage of punches that sent him down against the ropes toward the end of the round. Dauthuille was counted out with 13 seconds left in the fight.[[27]](#cite_note-fatlady-27) This fight was named [Fight of the Year](/wiki/Ring_Magazine_fights_of_the_year "Ring Magazine fights of the year") for 1950 by [*The Ring*](/wiki/The_Ring_(magazine) "The Ring (magazine)") magazine.

### Saint Valentine's Day Massacre of boxing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Saint Valentine's Day Massacre of boxing")]

Main article: [Jake LaMotta vs. Sugar Ray Robinson VI](/wiki/Jake_LaMotta_vs._Sugar_Ray_Robinson_VI "Jake LaMotta vs. Sugar Ray Robinson VI")

The sixth and final fight between LaMotta and Robinson took place at [Chicago Stadium](/wiki/Chicago_Stadium "Chicago Stadium"). This fight was scheduled for 15 rounds and was for the middleweight title.[[15]](#cite_note-Sweet_Thunder-15) Held on February 14, 1951, Saint Valentine's Day, the fight became known as boxing's version of the [Saint Valentine's Day Massacre](/wiki/Saint_Valentine%27s_Day_Massacre "Saint Valentine's Day Massacre"). In the last few rounds, LaMotta began to take a horrible beating and was soon unable to defend himself from Robinson's powerful blows. But LaMotta refused to go down. Robinson won by a [technical knockout](/wiki/Technical_knockout "Technical knockout") in the 13th round, when the fight was stopped.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Light heavyweight

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Light heavyweight")]

LaMotta moved up to [light heavyweight](/wiki/Light_heavyweight "Light heavyweight") after losing his world middleweight title. He had poor results at first. He lost his debut against [Bob Murphy](/wiki/Irish_Bob_Murphy "Irish Bob Murphy"), lost a split decision to Norman Hayes, and drew with Gene Hairston in his first three bouts. In his next three fights, LaMotta had rematches with Hayes, Hairston, and Murphy, and defeated all of them by unanimous decisions.

On December 31, 1952, LaMotta had his next fight against [Danny Nardico](/wiki/Danny_Nardico "Danny Nardico"). He knocked LaMotta down for the only time in his career (not counting his thrown 1947 fight) by a right hand in the seventh round. LaMotta got up and was beaten against a corner by Nardico until the bell rang. LaMotta's corner stopped the bout before the eighth round began.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Following that fight, LaMotta took time off; when he returned, in early 1954,[[30]](#cite_note-30) he knocked out his first two opponents, Johnny Pretzie (TKO 4) and Al McCoy (KO 1), but a controversial split decision loss to Billy Kilgore on April 14, 1954, convinced him to retire.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Post-boxing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Post-boxing")]

After retiring from the ring, LaMotta owned and managed a bar at 1120 Collins Ave in Miami Beach. He also became a stage actor and comedian. In 1958 he was arrested and charged with introducing men to an underage girl at a club he owned in Miami. He was convicted and served six months on a [chain gang](/wiki/Chain_gang "Chain gang"), although he maintained his innocence.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1960 LaMotta was called to testify before a [U.S. Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") [sub-committee](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Senate_committees "List of United States Senate committees") that was looking into underworld influence on boxing. He testified that he had thrown his bout with Billy Fox so that the mob would arrange a title bout for him.[[22]](#cite_note-ReelLife-22)

LaMotta appeared in more than 15 films, including *[The Hustler](/wiki/The_Hustler "The Hustler")* (1961) with [Paul Newman](/wiki/Paul_Newman "Paul Newman") and [Jackie Gleason](/wiki/Jackie_Gleason "Jackie Gleason"), in which he had a role as a bartender.[[32]](#cite_note-32) He appeared in several episodes of the [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") police comedy *[Car 54 Where Are You?](/wiki/Car_54_Where_Are_You%3F "Car 54 Where Are You?")* (1961–63). A lifelong [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") fan, he organized the Jake LaMotta All-Star Team in [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"). The LaMotta team played in [Sterling Oval](/wiki/Sterling_Oval "Sterling Oval") which was located between 165th and 164th Streets between Clay and Teller Avenue.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1965, LaMotta appeared as "Big Jule" in the [New York City Center](/wiki/New_York_City_Center "New York City Center") production of *[Guys and Dolls](/wiki/Guys_and_Dolls "Guys and Dolls")* for 15 performances alongside [Alan King](/wiki/Alan_King "Alan King") and [Jerry Orbach](/wiki/Jerry_Orbach "Jerry Orbach").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In his old age, LaMotta lived mostly in a northern suburb of Miami, FL, alternating to [Bisbee, Arizona](/wiki/Bisbee,_Arizona "Bisbee, Arizona") during the rainy Florida summer season,[[34]](#cite_note-34) where he knew comedian Doug Stanhope.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

## Fighting style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Fighting style")]

LaMotta is recognized as having had one of the best [chins](/wiki/Chin_(boxing) "Chin (boxing)") in boxing. He rolled with punches, minimizing their force and damage when they landed, but he was also able to absorb many blows.[[12]](#cite_note-AftertheFall-12) In the Saint Valentine's Day Massacre, his sixth bout with Robinson, LaMotta suffered numerous severe blows to the head. Commentators could be heard saying "No man can take this kind of punishment!" But LaMotta did not go down. The fight was stopped by the referee in the 13th round, declaring it a TKO victory for Robinson.

LaMotta was one of the first boxers to adopt the "bully" style of fighting, in that he always stayed close and in punching range of his opponent, by stalking him around the ring, and sacrificed taking punches himself in order to land his own shots. Due to his aggressive, unrelenting style he was known as "The Bronx Bull".[[36]](#cite_note-36) He boasted "No son-of-a-bitch ever knocked me off my feet", but that claim was ended in December 1952 at the hands of Danny Nardico when Nardico caught him with a hard right in the seventh round. LaMotta fell into the ropes and went down. After regaining his footing, he was unable to come out for the next round.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## *Raging Bull: My Story*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Raging Bull: My Story")]

*Raging Bull: My Story* is a 1970 second edition of LaMotta's memoir. The autobiographical details include his life as a young teenage criminal; his reformation in prison; his extensive career as an amateur and professional boxer; his struggles with organized crime who kept a boxing title out of reach; and his jealous obsession with his wife, Vikki. The book details his life, from childhood until the end of his fame.

The first edition is:

* La Motta, Jake with Carter, Joseph and Savage, Peter (1970). *Raging Bull: My Story*. Englewood Cliffs, N.J.: Prentice-Hall [1970]. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-13-752527-3](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-13-752527-3 "Special:BookSources/0-13-752527-3").

## *Raging Bull*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Raging Bull")]

Main article: [Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")

Hollywood executives approached LaMotta with the idea of a movie about his life, based on his 1970 memoir *[Raging Bull: My Story](/wiki/Raging_Bull:_My_Story "Raging Bull: My Story")*. The film, *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")*, released in 1980, was a [box-office bomb](/wiki/Box-office_bomb "Box-office bomb"), but eventually received overwhelming critical acclaim for both director [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") and actor [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro"), who gained about 60 pounds during the shooting of the film to play the older LaMotta in later scenes.

To accurately portray the younger LaMotta, De Niro trained with LaMotta until LaMotta felt he was ready to box professionally. De Niro lived in Paris for three months, eating at the finest restaurants in order to gain sufficient weight to portray LaMotta after retirement.[[22]](#cite_note-ReelLife-22) De Niro won the [Academy Award for Best Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor") for his performance.

## Later life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Later life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Jake_LaMotta_1980s.jpg/250px-Jake_LaMotta_1980s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jake_LaMotta_1980s.jpg)

LaMotta in the 1980s, with former wife [Vikki LaMotta](/wiki/Vikki_LaMotta "Vikki LaMotta"), right, actress [Cathy Moriarty](/wiki/Cathy_Moriarty "Cathy Moriarty"), left

LaMotta had a troubled personal life, including an early spell in a [reformatory](/wiki/Reformatory "Reformatory"), and was married seven times. He admitted to having raped a woman, having beaten his wives and coming close to beating a man to death during a robbery.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

In February 1998, LaMotta's elder son, Jake LaMotta Jr., died of liver cancer.[[12]](#cite_note-AftertheFall-12) In September 1998, his younger son, Joseph LaMotta, died aged 49 in the crash of [Swissair Flight 111](/wiki/Swissair_Flight_111 "Swissair Flight 111").[[12]](#cite_note-AftertheFall-12)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

His nephew John LaMotta fought in the [heavyweight](/wiki/Heavyweight "Heavyweight")-novice class of the 2001 [Golden Gloves](/wiki/Golden_Gloves "Golden Gloves") championship tournament.[[41]](#cite_note-41) John later became an actor, and one of his roles was as "Duke", who ran the bar of that name featured in the television comedy series *[Frasier](/wiki/Frasier "Frasier")*. Another nephew, [William Lustig](/wiki/William_Lustig "William Lustig"), is a well-known director and producer of horror films and the president of [Blue Underground](/wiki/Blue_Underground "Blue Underground"), Inc.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

LaMotta had four daughters, including Christi by his second wife [Vikki LaMotta](/wiki/Vikki_LaMotta "Vikki LaMotta") and Stephanie by his fourth wife Dimitria. He married his seventh wife, his longtime fiancée Denise Baker, on January 4, 2013.[[43]](#cite_note-43) LaMotta remained active on the speaking and autograph circuit, and published several books about his career, his life, and his fights with Robinson.

LaMotta appeared in a 50-minute New York City stage production, *Lady and the Champ*, in July 2012. The production focused on LaMotta's boxing career, and was criticized by *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* as poorly executed and a "bizarre debacle".[[44]](#cite_note-Jaworowski-44)

LaMotta is the subject of a documentary directed and produced by Greg Olliver. The film features an appearance by [Mike Tyson](/wiki/Mike_Tyson "Mike Tyson") among other notable athletes, actors and Jake's family and friends. Also in production was a sequel to *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")*, although [MGM](/wiki/MGM "MGM") filed suit to halt the project, saying that LaMotta did not have the right to make a sequel. The lawsuit was settled on July 31, 2012, when LaMotta agreed to change the title of the film to *[The Bronx Bull](/wiki/The_Bronx_Bull "The Bronx Bull")*.[[45]](#cite_note-The_Hollywood_Reporter-45)

*LaMotta: The Bronx Bull* stars actor [William Forsythe](/wiki/William_Forsythe_(actor) "William Forsythe (actor)") as LaMotta, while [Paul Sorvino](/wiki/Paul_Sorvino "Paul Sorvino") plays his father. It also features [Joe Mantegna](/wiki/Joe_Mantegna "Joe Mantegna"), [Tom Sizemore](/wiki/Tom_Sizemore "Tom Sizemore"), [Penelope Ann Miller](/wiki/Penelope_Ann_Miller "Penelope Ann Miller"), [Natasha Henstridge](/wiki/Natasha_Henstridge "Natasha Henstridge"), [Joey Diaz](/wiki/Joey_Diaz "Joey Diaz") and [Ray Wise](/wiki/Ray_Wise "Ray Wise").[[46]](#cite_note-46)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jake_LaMotta&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Death")]

LaMotta died on September 19, 2017, from complications of pneumonia in a nursing home in Florida, at the age of 95.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[8]](#cite_note-nyt-8)[[11]](#cite_note-ausobit-11)
